storytellers.bio: Collective voice of the Flexi school group.
Young person at the first BG Fit camp.
Young person from Doomadgee at BG Fit camp.

Uncle George Leon, a distinguished Kalkadoon elder, embodies resilience and community spirit in Mount Isa, where his roots run deep since his family's arrival in 1947. Raised in a vibrant household that fostered camaraderie among thousands, including local rugby league players, Uncle George learned the value of community early on. Known for his 'tough love' approach, he has dedicated his life to steering youth towards education, emphasizing the importance of engaging with children in school to curb crime and nurture potential. His unique role as a PLO was instrumental in connecting with young people and reinforcing community trust, making Uncle George a beloved and respected figure whose impact spans generations.

Brodie Germain is a proud Pita Pita Wayaka man from Mount Isa, whose journey weaves together his roots in remote Aboriginal communities and his current role in child protection. Juggling a fitness business, he dedicates his early mornings and evenings to building a better future for himself and his community, all while navigating the challenges of a life lived between tradition and modernity. With the heart of a storyteller, Brodie embraces every opportunity to share his experiences, grateful for connections that empower him to step beyond familiar boundaries. His path, marked by resilience and gratitude, is a testament to the strength of Indigenous identity and the importance of community in shaping one's life.

profiles.bio: Collective voice of the Flexi school group — 20 boys aged 10-17.

An inspiring voice in community storytelling, the Mount Isa Camp Participant played a pivotal role in the inaugural Mount Isa Bush Camp, a transformative experience for twelve young boys. By immersing them in the natural world without modern distractions, he facilitated a powerful reconnection to culture and identity. Through storytelling and mentorship, he helped participants embrace challenges, fostering resilience and camaraderie. The camp featured a Welcome to Country by an Elder, highlighting the significance of local songlines and traditions. With a passion for youth empowerment, he continues to engage with participants through regular gym sessions, demonstrating the lasting impact of shared experiences and community spirit.
Doomadgee Camp Participant shares valuable insights and experiences through their storytelling.
Spinifex Res Youth shares valuable insights and experiences through their storytelling.

Brodie Germain is a proud Pita Pita Wayaka man from Mount Isa, whose journey weaves together his roots in remote Aboriginal communities and his current role in child protection. Juggling a fitness business, he dedicates his early mornings and evenings to building a better future for himself and his community, all while navigating the challenges of a life lived between tradition and modernity. With the heart of a storyteller, Brodie embraces every opportunity to share his experiences, grateful for connections that empower him to step beyond familiar boundaries. His path, marked by resilience and gratitude, is a testament to the strength of Indigenous identity and the importance of community in shaping one's life.

Uncle George Leon, a distinguished Kalkadoon elder, embodies resilience and community spirit in Mount Isa, where his roots run deep since his family's arrival in 1947. Raised in a vibrant household that fostered camaraderie among thousands, including local rugby league players, Uncle George learned the value of community early on. Known for his 'tough love' approach, he has dedicated his life to steering youth towards education, emphasizing the importance of engaging with children in school to curb crime and nurture potential. His unique role as a PLO was instrumental in connecting with young people and reinforcing community trust, making Uncle George a beloved and respected figure whose impact spans generations.

Gracie Ryder is a dedicated police liaison officer from Mount Isa, currently serving as the hub coordinator for the Youth Responder team. With a passion for youth empowerment, she actively engages with local children, focusing on preventing offending and promoting education. Gracie plays a vital role during Youth Week events, facilitating community connections that foster safety and support. Her deep-rooted understanding of the challenges faced by local youth, combined with her belief in the transformative power of sports and education, makes her an invaluable advocate for positive change. Gracie’s commitment to building rapport and ongoing support systems highlights her as a respected voice in her community.

Siva is a passionate youth worker at Injalinji, dedicated to empowering young people through case management and community engagement. With a focus on connecting youth to essential services such as Centrelink and educational opportunities, Siva plays a pivotal role in fostering a supportive environment for Aboriginal culture. Their involvement in events like NAIDOC Week showcases a commitment to cultural education, helping young individuals rediscover their heritage while enjoying community activities and diverse culinary experiences. Siva's work not only aids in personal development but also strengthens community bonds, making them a vital voice in advocating for the rights and growth of Indigenous youth.

Rashad Gavin Isaacson is a passionate storyteller and dancer from Mount Isa, dedicated to celebrating and preserving Indigenous culture through art. As a member of the Kalkadoon Sundowners dance crew, Rashad plays a pivotal role in NAIDOC Week celebrations, where he showcases the richness of his heritage and encourages young people to engage with their cultural roots. He believes in the power of dance as a medium for connection and expression, advocating for community initiatives that inspire youth to embrace their identity. Rashad's commitment to cultural education and youth empowerment makes him a vital voice in his community, fostering pride and unity among the next generation.

Aunty Ivy is a passionate storyteller and cultural ambassador dedicated to celebrating and preserving Aboriginal culture. With a lifelong commitment to the NAIDOC March, she has become a vital voice in her community, ensuring that both Aboriginal and non-Aboriginal people come together to honor and respect Indigenous traditions. Through her work, Aunty Ivy emphasizes the importance of leading by example, inspiring the younger generation to appreciate their heritage and respect their elders. Her initiatives focus on cultural education, community engagement, and fostering intergenerational connections. Aunty Ivy's unique perspective enriches her storytelling, making her an invaluable asset to the community, where she advocates for inclusivity and understanding across cultures.

Nayden is a vibrant young storyteller from Mount Isa, whose candid insights reflect the realities of youth in his community. With a keen understanding of local life, Nayden sheds light on the challenges faced by young people, including boredom and mischief, while also expressing a desire for connection with nature. His experiences at Spinifex School, where he enjoys spending time with friends and engaging in activities like gym workouts, showcase his commitment to personal growth and community involvement. Nayden's unique perspective as a youth in Mount Isa makes him a valuable voice for his peers, advocating for positive engagement and outdoor exploration, and inspiring others to connect with their surroundings and each other.

The YPA Team is a dedicated group of storytellers and community leaders committed to fostering cultural pride and connection among Indigenous youth. Through their Intensive on Country program in Mount Isa, they create transformative experiences that empower young people to engage with their culture and community. NAIDOC Week serves as a vital platform for the team, symbolizing unity and celebration of Indigenous heritage. Their efforts focus on teaching the next generation about their roots, instilling a sense of belonging and pride. With a deep respect for their community, the YPA Team not only uplifts voices but also shapes the future by nurturing cultural awareness and resilience among the youth they serve.
Benji - Young Person shares valuable insights and experiences through their storytelling.

Luigi is a vibrant youth advocate and storyteller from Mount Isa, bringing a fresh perspective on youth engagement and health. With a passion for fitness and well-being, he emphasizes the importance of physical strength in fostering self-esteem among young people. Luigi actively participates in community initiatives, such as the Youth Hub and Headspace, where he engages with peers to promote positive lifestyles. His experiences at Spinifex school, where he excels in math and English, showcase his commitment to education. Through his storytelling, Luigi inspires fellow youth to embrace their strengths and contribute to their community, making him a valuable voice in shaping the future of Mount Isa.

Meet Jay, a vibrant young storyteller from Australia whose unique perspective offers a glimpse into the everyday lives of his peers. With a knack for engaging narratives, Jay captures the essence of youth culture, exploring themes of identity, adventure, and community. His love for the outdoors shines through as he recounts tales of going 'bush'—hunting, riding, and connecting with nature—highlighting the importance of these experiences in fostering resilience and joy among young people. Jay advocates for local community initiatives, encouraging peers to embrace outdoor activities that promote camaraderie and well-being. Through his storytelling, he serves as a vital voice for the youth, illustrating their struggles and triumphs with humor and authenticity.

Nigel Tain is a passionate advocate for youth engagement and community development in Mount Isa, serving as the Vice President of Men's Rugby Union. With a focus on promoting positive opportunities for young people, Nigel leads initiatives that celebrate cultural heritage, such as NAIDOC events and youth rugby programs. His commitment to supporting local youth is evident in his efforts to provide avenues for sport and mentorship, believing in the power of community connection. By facilitating exciting opportunities like the Under 16 boys' trip to the Western Sevens, he empowers the next generation to aspire for greatness. Through his dedication, Nigel embodies the spirit of resilience and hope, transforming perceptions of Mount Isa into a place of potential and community pride.

Danielle Germaine is a passionate storyteller and a dedicated advocate for cultural heritage, particularly within the Indigenous community. As a proud mother and active participant in NAIDOC celebrations, Danielle emphasizes the importance of family, connection, and cultural pride. Her work revolves around fostering community ties and encouraging younger generations to embrace their identity. Through various storytelling initiatives, she seeks to share the rich narratives of her culture, helping to bridge gaps between generations and promote understanding. Danielle’s commitment to community engagement and her role as a cultural mentor make her a vital voice in the celebration and preservation of Indigenous stories and traditions.

Ben, a dedicated social worker from Canada, has recently joined the Gidgie Healing Aboriginal Medical Service in Mount Isa, where he actively contributes to fostering community spirit during NAIDOC week. With seven years of experience working with youth, Ben is passionate about creating safe spaces for young people to express themselves freely, helping them navigate their challenges. His commitment to community well-being is evident through his engagement in local events, where he connects with clients and promotes valuable resources. Ben's unique perspective enriches the community, making him a vital voice in supporting youth and strengthening cultural ties in a diverse environment.
Uncle Warren King shares valuable insights and experiences through their storytelling.

stories.title: Danielle Germaine. - Recording
stories.summary: Danielle Germaine, Brodie's mother, reflects on celebrating NAIDOC with family while emphasizing the cultural pride she instilled in her children from infancy. A brief, powerful mention of Brodie being removed from her at four hours old hints at early trauma, yet the overall tone celebrates family resilience, connection, and the ongoing importance of community gathering during NAIDOC.
stories.content: Danielle Germaine, Brodie's mother, reflects on celebrating NAIDOC with family while emphasizing the cultural pride she instilled in her children from infancy. A brief, powerful mention of Brodie being removed from her at four hours old hints at early trauma, yet the overall tone celebrates family resilience, connection, and the ongoing importance of community gathering during NAIDOC.

stories.title: Jay - Young Person interview
stories.summary: Jay is a young person experiencing boredom and behavioral challenges in an urban environment, drawn to conflict and negative peer influences. However, he demonstrates clear self-awareness and shows marked enthusiasm and positive engagement when discussing bush activities, particularly hunting and connection to country, recognizing these experiences keep him out of trouble.
stories.content: Jay is a young person experiencing boredom and behavioral challenges in an urban environment, drawn to conflict and negative peer influences. However, he demonstrates clear self-awareness and shows marked enthusiasm and positive engagement when discussing bush activities, particularly hunting and connection to country, recognizing these experiences keep him out of trouble.

stories.title: Nigel - Recording
stories.summary: Nigel Tain, Vice President of Men's Rugby Union in Mount Isa, discusses his work engaging Indigenous youth through sport and cultural activities. He challenges negative media portrayals of Mount Isa youth, emphasizes the importance of combining sport with on-country cultural experiences, and celebrates historic milestones like taking junior players to their first away tournament. His approach integrates rugby union with cultural connection, mentorship, and community collaboration.
stories.content: Nigel Tain, Vice President of Men's Rugby Union in Mount Isa, discusses his work engaging Indigenous youth through sport and cultural activities. He challenges negative media portrayals of Mount Isa youth, emphasizes the importance of combining sport with on-country cultural experiences, and celebrates historic milestones like taking junior players to their first away tournament. His approach integrates rugby union with cultural connection, mentorship, and community collaboration.

stories.title: Uncle Warren King - Recording
stories.summary: Uncle Warren King, a Kalkadoon elder, reflects on NAIDOC as an intergenerational legacy passed from ancestors to future leaders. Drawing on his mother's founding role in key Indigenous organizations and his own activism beginning at age 17, he emphasizes the importance of individualized, trauma-informed approaches to supporting Indigenous youth and maintaining cultural continuity.
stories.content: Uncle Warren King, a Kalkadoon elder, reflects on NAIDOC as an intergenerational legacy passed from ancestors to future leaders. Drawing on his mother's founding role in key Indigenous organizations and his own activism beginning at age 17, he emphasizes the importance of individualized, trauma-informed approaches to supporting Indigenous youth and maintaining cultural continuity.

stories.title: Gracie Ryder - Youth Police
stories.summary: Gracie Ryder, a Police Liaison Officer and Youth Corresponder hub coordinator in Mount Isa, discusses her work diverting young people from offending through relationship-building, education, and sport. She emphasizes Mount Isa's strong communal values, the importance of role models with lived experience like her longtime friend Brodie, and the power of cross-service collaboration to support vulnerable youth.
stories.content: Gracie Ryder, a Police Liaison Officer and Youth Corresponder hub coordinator in Mount Isa, discusses her work diverting young people from offending through relationship-building, education, and sport. She emphasizes Mount Isa's strong communal values, the importance of role models with lived experience like her longtime friend Brodie, and the power of cross-service collaboration to support vulnerable youth.

themes.name: Education and sport as protective factors

stories.title: Benji - Youth Day Yarn
stories.summary: Benji, a young person from Mount Isa, shares what he gets up to with his mates — walking around, playing footy, hanging at the bowling alley — and why he trusts Brodie at BG Fit. In his own voice, Benji describes Brodie's cultural authority and the family-scale activities (fishing, hunting) that BG Fit enables. Natural speech preserved from recorded interview; this replaces a prior summary-only ingest.
stories.content:
[00:00:00]

Hi bra. Tell us your name and spell it. My name is Benji. B-E-E-N-J-I-K-I. And where are you from? I'm from Mount Isa. Tell me about yourself. Yeah, just walk around, with all the boys. Yeah, just join ourself. Just walk around. Go play footy sometime in the park, or we do kick around me and the boys.

Oh, sometime we come to the bowling alley, and thing what's going on in Tiani? Do people get in trouble or what's that? Sometimes people breaking in and all that. And it's got my voice, Roddy, why do you recognize that video? For this one? Yeah. What's, oh shit, Rowan. Yeah. What do you need to not do that shit?

You reckon? What's some things that help you not to stop getting in trouble? I don't know. Work on some of East Way. Yeah. Yeah. Tell me about Brodie. Brodie, you got a big gym. Yeah. And we'll be going there five o'clock, do [00:01:00] some workout. Why you wanna hang out with Brodie in the gym and stuff?

Just because he got to big gym and work out, yeah. Do you reckon he can help you within your whole life? If you need help, would you trust Brady to help you out? He right. That big fell. Yeah. Yeah. He know half of the black people. That's why. Yeah. He need been around the world.

He been seeing pack of black people, now half of our family know that we've been fishing with them, been hunting, been going, doing all the fun stuff with all our family, come back that way. Yeah, that's wicked. What else you wanna say? That's it mate. Is that you sure? Yeah. I see. Hey, lemme take a photo of you ready?

What someone like be famous, did you? Wait a minute.

Watch. I'm famous like daddy, famous, like I'm famous, like da dirty. [00:02:00] Who else? Who else wants the ya? Me like him named my bloke. Looks dirty.

Else want you on? Nah, nah me. Not kidding. No Waiting for priority.

stories.title: Siva - Recording
stories.summary: Siva is a youth worker at Injalinji who provides practical support to Indigenous young people including Centrelink assistance, employment help, and boarding school placements. He values NAIDOC Week as a crucial opportunity for youth to connect with their culture, noting that many young people lack knowledge of their identity and need these immersive cultural experiences.
stories.content: Siva is a youth worker at Injalinji who provides practical support to Indigenous young people including Centrelink assistance, employment help, and boarding school placements. He values NAIDOC Week as a crucial opportunity for youth to connect with their culture, noting that many young people lack knowledge of their identity and need these immersive cultural experiences.

stories.title: Rashad Gavin Isaacson - Recording
stories.summary: Rashad Gavin Isaacson, a young Kalkadoon dancer with the Sundowners dance crew, shares his participation in NAIDOC Week celebrations in Mount Isa. He expresses that NAIDOC and culture are 'everything' to him, and advocates for engaging more young people through showing them their culture and encouraging (not forcing) participation in cultural dance and activities.
stories.content: Rashad Gavin Isaacson, a young Kalkadoon dancer with the Sundowners dance crew, shares his participation in NAIDOC Week celebrations in Mount Isa. He expresses that NAIDOC and culture are 'everything' to him, and advocates for engaging more young people through showing them their culture and encouraging (not forcing) participation in cultural dance and activities.

stories.title: Ben - Recording
stories.summary: Ben, a Canadian social worker in his first week at Gidgie Healing Aboriginal Medical Service in Mount Isa, shares his positive first impressions of the community during NAIDOC Week. Drawing on seven years of youth work experience, he emphasizes the importance of being a 'safe place' for young people - a non-judgmental space where they can express themselves freely and simply be kids without criticism from authority figures.
stories.content: Ben, a Canadian social worker in his first week at Gidgie Healing Aboriginal Medical Service in Mount Isa, shares his positive first impressions of the community during NAIDOC Week. Drawing on seven years of youth work experience, he emphasizes the importance of being a 'safe place' for young people - a non-judgmental space where they can express themselves freely and simply be kids without criticism from authority figures.

stories.title: YPA Team - Recording
stories.summary: The YPA (Youth Programs Australia) Intensive on Country team in Mount Isa describes NAIDOC Week as a time for community solidarity, cultural pride, and collective celebration of Indigenous achievements. Their youth program takes young people onto country to experientially teach cultural connection and community belonging.
stories.content: The YPA (Youth Programs Australia) Intensive on Country team in Mount Isa describes NAIDOC Week as a time for community solidarity, cultural pride, and collective celebration of Indigenous achievements. Their youth program takes young people onto country to experientially teach cultural connection and community belonging.

stories.title: Aunty Ivy - Recording
stories.summary: Aunty Ivy shares her lifelong commitment to NAIDOC celebrations, having never missed a march since childhood regardless of where she is in the country. She describes NAIDOC as both a celebration of Aboriginal culture and an opportunity for respectful cross-cultural engagement, emphasizing the importance of leading by example and teaching young people to respect and honour Elders as the foundation for cultural continuity.
stories.content: Aunty Ivy shares her lifelong commitment to NAIDOC celebrations, having never missed a march since childhood regardless of where she is in the country. She describes NAIDOC as both a celebration of Aboriginal culture and an opportunity for respectful cross-cultural engagement, emphasizing the importance of leading by example and teaching young people to respect and honour Elders as the foundation for cultural continuity.

stories.title: Spinifex Residential — Getting Through to the Hard Cases
stories.summary: BG Fit's program at Spinifex Residential works with young people in out-of-home care and the justice system, using sport to build trust.
stories.content:
Spinifex Residential in Mount Isa is where some of our toughest kids end up. These are young people who've been removed from home, who've been through the justice system, some of them multiple times. They're angry, they're hurt, and most of them don't trust adults.

When we first started going in there, the staff were sceptical. They'd seen programs come and go. The kids would test us — not listening, walking off, trying to get a rise. But we kept coming back.

After about the fourth session, something changed. The kids started waiting for us at the gate. They'd have their shoes on, ready to go. One boy who wouldn't even look at us in week one was leading the warm-up by week six.

The residential staff told us the kids are calmer on the days we come. There's less incidents. They sleep better. It's like they need somewhere to put all that energy and emotion, and the gym gives them that.

We don't lecture them. We don't ask about their charges or their history. We just train with them. We treat them like young athletes, not like problems. I think that's what makes the difference.

One of the boys there, he wants to get into boxing. We're trying to connect him with a boxing gym for when he gets out. That's the pathway — not just keeping them busy while they're inside, but connecting them to something on the outside.

stories.title: Tuesday Gym Sessions — Building Consistency
stories.summary: BG Fit's weekly Tuesday gym sessions at Mount Isa PCYC have grown from 5 to 20 regular attendees, building trust through consistency.
stories.content:
Every Tuesday we're at the PCYC in Mount Isa. It started small — maybe five kids showing up. Now we consistently get 15 to 20.

The sessions aren't just about lifting weights or running laps. We start with a yarn. How was your week? What's going on at school? Anyone got anything they need to talk about? Then we train.

Consistency is the key. These kids have had so many people come and go in their lives. Programs start and then disappear. Workers come for six months and leave. So when we keep showing up, week after week, that builds trust.

One young fella — he was getting into trouble every week. Fighting at school, not going home. His mum was at her wit's end. She brought him to us and said 'Just try.' That was eight weeks ago. He hasn't been in trouble since. He's there every Tuesday, first one through the door.

It's not magic. It's just showing up. Being consistent. Giving them something to look forward to. A lot of these kids don't have that.

We're looking at expanding to a second night — maybe Thursdays. The demand is there. We just need the funding to keep the PCYC booking and cover equipment costs. The Kickstarter grant from BHP helps but it's tight.

stories.title: Doomadgee Camp — Taking It Remote
stories.summary: BG Fit's Doomadgee bush camp brought fitness and cultural activities to one of Queensland's most remote Indigenous communities.
stories.content:
Doomadgee was different. It's remote — really remote. When we drove in, some of the boys from Mount Isa couldn't believe how far it was. But that's where our mob are, and that's where we need to be.

The community welcomed us with open arms. The Elders were so happy to see someone bringing activities for the young people. They said no one comes out here. No one runs programs. The kids just have nothing.

We set up at the oval and within an hour we had 30 kids there. Word spread fast. They were hungry for it — hungry for someone to show up and do something with them.

The camp ran for two days. We did fitness sessions in the morning, cultural activities in the afternoon with local Elders, and sports in the evening when it cooled down. The kids loved it. Some of them followed us everywhere we went.

What hit me hardest was when one of the local women said to me: 'Please come back. These kids need this.' That's why we do what we do. It's not about us. It's about showing up in communities that have been forgotten.

We're planning to go back quarterly. The Sport and Rec grant will help us cover the travel costs. Doomadgee is 500km from Mount Isa — it's not cheap to get there, but it's where we're needed most.

stories.title: Mount Isa Bush Camp — First One on Country
stories.summary: The first BG Fit bush camp took 12 young people on country near Mount Isa for three days of cultural connection and Elder guidance.
stories.content:
The Mount Isa bush camp was our first big event. We took 12 young fellas out on country for three days. No phones, no internet — just the bush, the fire, and each other.

Day one was hard. Some of these kids had never been out of town before. They didn't know how to set up a swag, didn't know what to do without their phones. But by the second night, something shifted. They were helping each other, sharing stories around the fire, asking the Elders questions about country.

We had Uncle come out and do a Welcome to Country. He told the boys about the old tracks, the songlines that run through this area. You could see it clicking for some of them — this connection to something bigger than themselves.

The camp wasn't just about getting kids out bush. It was about showing them they can do hard things. Some of these boys had never spent a night away from home. By the end of it, they didn't want to leave.

One boy told me afterwards: 'That was the best three days of my life.' He's now one of our regulars at Tuesday gym. That's the ripple effect.

stories.title: Why I Started BG Fit
stories.summary: Brodie shares the founding story of BG Fit — born from witnessing too many Indigenous young people cycling through the justice system without support.
stories.content:
I started BG Fit because I saw too many of our mob going through the justice system and not getting the support they needed. Growing up in Mount Isa, I saw what happened when young fellas had nothing to do — they'd get into trouble. But when they had sport, when they had something to work towards, everything changed.

I played rugby league at a high level and I know what sport can do for young people. It's not just about fitness — it's about discipline, respect, turning up when you said you would. These are the things that keep our mob strong.

We started with just a gym session on Tuesdays at the PCYC. Five kids turned up the first week. Now we've got 15-20 regulars and the word is spreading. The parents are seeing changes in their kids — better behaviour at school, more confidence, showing up on time.

The goal was never just fitness. It's about creating a pathway. Some of these kids have never had anyone believe in them before. When you show up every week and you're there for them, that means something. That's what BG Fit is about.
